text:
When *Babylon* first opened in late 2022, its critical reception was mixed rather than outright disastrous. A lot of reviewers admired its ambition, visual energy, performances, and sheer excess, but many others thought it was overlong, self-indulgent, and too eager to shock. So the early response was polarized: some critics saw it as a bold, feverish Hollywood epic, while others found it messy and exhausting.

In aggregate terms, it landed in the “mixed” range on the major review sites when reviews first came out. That means it was not embraced as a clear critical success, but it also was not universally panned. The conversation around it was especially intense because Damien Chazelle had come off highly acclaimed films, so expectations were very high and the split response felt more dramatic.

A fair summary is that *Babylon* opened to divisive reviews: praised passionately by some critics, rejected strongly by others, and discussed as a big swing that did not produce consensus. If you want, I can also give you a more specific breakdown of what the positive reviews praised and what the negative reviews objected to.
